name: Ken Forkish Same-Day Straight Pizza Dough
description: Ken Forkish's same-day straight pizza dough (from Flour Water Salt Yeast), via The Cooking of Joy. A no-knead, fold-built 70% hydration dough that mixes in the morning and bakes that evening — crisp, blistered, airy crust, no oil. Small batch makes two ~10-inch pizzas; scale linearly for more.
recipeIngredient: 5 g (about 1 tsp) fine sea salt or kosher salt
recipeInstructions: Combine the flour, salt, and instant yeast in a tub or large bowl. Add the warm water (90-95°F). Mix by hand — squeezing and folding — until no dry flour remains and you have a sticky, cohesive dough, about 3-5 minutes. Cover.

In the first hour or so, give the dough 2-3 stretch-and-folds: wet your hand, lift one side of the dough up, stretch it, and fold it over onto itself; rotate and repeat a few times. Cover between folds.
Bulk-rise at warm room temperature (~70°F) until roughly doubled to tripled and full of bubbles, about 5-6 hours (faster in a warmer kitchen).
Flour the counter, turn the dough out, and divide into 2 equal pieces. Shape each into a tight ball, tucking the edges underneath to build surface tension.
Rest the balls, covered and lightly floured, 20-60 minutes before shaping (or refrigerate and bring back to room temp later).
Stretch each ball by hand to about 10 inches, top, and bake on a stone or steel as hot as your oven goes (550°F+), or in a pizza oven, until the crust is browned and blistered.
author: Ken Forkish (Flour Water Salt Yeast), via The Cooking of Joy

ai.cliffNotes: No-knead, fold-built same-day pizza dough at 70% hydration. Mix in ~5 min, do a few folds in the first hour, bulk-rise 5-6 h, ball, rest, and bake screaming hot. Two ~10-inch pizzas; no oil.
keyTips: The warm 90-95°F water is the engine of the same-day timeline — it keeps the dough warm enough to ferment fully in 5-6 hours. In a cool kitchen, find a warm spot or expect it to take longer.
Don't skip the early stretch-and-folds — they build gluten strength without kneading (the whole point of Forkish's method) and give you an open, airy crumb.
It's a wet 70% dough and will be sticky — keep your hands and counter floured or damp, and resist working in lots of extra flour, which makes a denser, tougher crust.
No oil + a long ferment = crisp, flavorful, blistered crust. Bake as hot as possible on a fully preheated stone or steel.
To scale up, keep the ratios (flour 100% / water 70% / salt 2% / yeast 0.2%); Forkish's full book recipe is ~1000 g flour for five larger dough balls.
